narrative: A well-architected 9-stage HITL workflow with excellent progressive disclosure, consistent stage templates, and correct intelligence placement. The primary opportunity is closing the gap between what stages produce and what the assembly script outputs — users invest effort in stages 5–8 that doesn't fully materialize in the final package. Secondary concerns are Stage 4 scaling for larger orgs and under-specified compaction recovery.

opportunities.name: Deferred Validation — Errors Only Caught at Stage 9
opportunities.description: All automated validation is concentrated in the final stage. Eight stages of data accumulation rely solely on the LLM for integrity checks. Errors discovered during assembly require expensive backtracking through potentially hours of prior work.
opportunities.impact: Catches structural errors at the stage they're introduced instead of at assembly time. Eliminates costly multi-stage backtracking and reduces LLM tax on deterministic checks by ~4,200–12,100 tokens per run.
opportunities.action: Create a unified validate-draft.py script that validates the draft JSON at any stage transition, with progressive checks (org tree integrity at stage ≥3, budget arithmetic at stage ≥7, cron syntax at stage ≥8). Call it after each stage gate approval.

findings.title: All validation concentrated at Stage 9
findings.detail: No automated integrity checks run during stages 1–8. The LLM is the sole validator for 8 stages of data accumulation.

findings.title: No draft JSON schema validation at stage transitions
findings.detail: Draft JSON well-formedness, required fields, and currentStage consistency are pure structure checks that should be scripted, not left to the LLM.

findings.title: Org chart tree integrity could validate 6 stages earlier
findings.detail: CEO uniqueness, orphan detection, cycle detection, and role enum validation are all available at Stage 3 but currently only checked at Stage 9.

findings.title: No centralized draft schema contract
findings.detail: Each stage independently defines what it writes to the draft. Over time, stages could drift (e.g., renaming a field) without assembly logic updating.

findings.title: validate-package.py missing cross-ref checks for goals/projects/routines
findings.detail: Goal ownerAgentId, project leadAgentId, and routine assigneeAgentId are not validated against the agent roster.

findings.title: validate-package.py has no cycle detection
findings.detail: Validates that reportsTo references exist but doesn't check for circular references (A → B → A) in the hierarchy.

findings.title: Hardcoded role enum in validator
findings.detail: Role enum (ceo, manager, engineer, designer, marketer, general) is hardcoded. Adding roles requires code changes. Should read from domain config.

opportunities.name: Assembly Output Gap — Package Doesn't Reflect All Stage Work
opportunities.description: The assembly script creates projects/, skills/, and tasks/ directories but never populates them. Users invest effort designing goals, discovering skills, and configuring budgets/routines in stages 5–8, but this data only partially materializes in the final package. A permissions field is referenced in Stage 4 with no backing schema or output.
opportunities.impact: Closing this gap ensures the final package reflects the full scope of user decisions, eliminating confusion about empty directories and preventing data from being silently dropped.
opportunities.action: Either update assemble-package.py to emit project files, skill references, and routine configurations, or explicitly document in stage instructions that these entities are runtime-seeded (not file-packaged) and remove the empty directories.

findings.title: skills/ directory created but never populated
findings.detail: Stage 9 lists skills/ in package output, but assembly script creates an empty directory. Skills are embedded in AGENTS.md frontmatter, making the directory misleading.

findings.title: projects/ directory created but never populated
findings.detail: Stage 6 designs projects with lead agents and goal alignment, but assembly only writes company and agent files.

findings.title: tasks/ directory created but never populated
findings.detail: The domain schema supports issues/tasks with identifiers, but assembly creates an empty tasks/ directory.

findings.title: Draft routines and budgets not written to package files
findings.detail: Draft contains routines and budgets from stages 7-8, but assembly doesn't write them to any file. User work is silently dropped.

findings.title: Permissions ghost field
findings.detail: Stage 4 references 'Permissions — what resources and tools this agent can access' in its proposal template, but no domain schema, assembly output, or validation exists for permissions.

findings.title: orgChart field in draft not in domain schema
findings.detail: Stage 3 Draft Update adds orgChart text field for ASCII visualization, but paperclip-domain.md does not define this field on the Company entity. Benign but inconsistent.

opportunities.name: Compaction Recovery and Session Continuity Under-Specified
opportunities.description: The draft JSON stores currentStage but not intra-stage progress. Recovery has no UX choreography, no tiered summary depth, and no 'save and quit' messaging. Two design principles from SKILL.md are never reinforced in stage files, making them vulnerable to context loss. There is no mechanism for revisiting earlier decisions.
opportunities.impact: Improving recovery and continuity reduces abandonment risk in a 30–60+ minute workflow, ensures design principles survive compaction, and gives users confidence their work persists across sessions.
opportunities.action: Add stageProgress to draft JSON for intra-stage state. Write recovery preamble patterns in the Compaction Recovery section. Add 'Your progress is saved' messaging at every stage gate. Add one-line principle reminders to each stage's Progression section.

findings.title: No intra-stage progress tracking in draft JSON
findings.detail: If compaction occurs mid-Stage 4 (after configuring 3 of 7 agents), the agent only knows 'Stage 4' — not which agents were completed. Lost progress must be re-done.

findings.title: No recovery UX choreography or preamble templates
findings.detail: Recovery instructions say 'read draft, resume' but don't specify what the agent says, how much to summarize, or whether to offer adjustment before resuming forward.

findings.title: No save-and-quit messaging at stage gates
findings.detail: Users are never told their progress persists. For a 9-stage workflow, users who need to leave feel they're abandoning work.

findings.title: Capture-don't-interrupt principle not reinforced in stage files
findings.detail: The principle is declared in SKILL.md Design Principles but never mentioned in any of the 9 stage reference files. It relies entirely on SKILL.md surviving compaction.

findings.title: Soft-gate phrasing not echoed in stage progressions
findings.detail: The 'Anything to adjust, or shall we move on?' pattern is in SKILL.md but no stage file includes it. Stages just say 'User approves → next.'

findings.title: No backward navigation mechanism
findings.detail: No mechanism for 'actually, I want to redo the org chart.' Budget implications in Stage 7 frequently prompt users to rethink agent count from Stage 3.

opportunities.name: Stage 4 Agent Configuration Doesn't Scale
opportunities.description: Stage 4 processes agents one-by-one with individual HITL approval. For a 10-agent org, this creates 10 sequential review cycles with no batch option, no progress indicator, and no template reuse. This is the primary abandonment point — users rubber-stamp by agent 5.
opportunities.impact: Adding batch approval and progress awareness cuts Stage 4 duration by 50%+ for medium orgs and prevents the review fatigue that drives first-timer abandonment.
opportunities.action: Add batch-approval mode: present CEO solo, managers as a group, same-role workers as a batch with per-agent override. Show progress indicators. Offer 'apply this template to all [engineers]' after the first of a role type.

findings.title: Per-agent sequential review doesn't scale
findings.detail: For a 10-agent org, Stage 4 involves 10 review-approve cycles. The skill provides no batch processing, role templates, or scale-adapted approach.

findings.title: No batch-approval option for similar-role agents
findings.detail: Four engineers with similar configs each get individual review. No 'approve all engineers' or 'apply template' mechanism exists.

findings.title: No progress indicator during per-agent walkthrough
findings.detail: Users have no sense of how many agents remain. No 'Agent 3 of 10' or percentage completion shown.

findings.title: Progression gate implicit for multi-agent iteration
findings.detail: Progression says 'All agents approved →' without specifying how the agent tracks which individual agents have been approved.

findings.title: First-timer drop-off risk by agent 5+
findings.detail: First-time users don't understand technical config details. By agent 5 of 8, they're rubber-stamping without reviewing. Tiered attention (CEO detailed, workers batched) would help.

opportunities.name: Minor Cross-Stage Prompt Duplication
opportunities.description: ~210 tokens of prunable content scattered across stages 5, 7, 8, and 9 — inferable guidance that the LLM would follow naturally, cross-stage duplication of model-tier advice, and validation checks that duplicate script logic. Individually negligible (2.4% of total content), but collectively a clean optimization.
opportunities.impact: Trimming ~210 tokens tightens the prompt surface and removes minor confusion about which stage owns model-selection guidance. Adding a config header improves scanner and tooling compatibility.
opportunities.action: Collapse Stage 5 search strategy to one line, trim Stage 7 cost optimization to reference Stage 4's model-tier guidance, replace Stage 9 validation check list with 'run script and report results.' Add type/interaction-model/stage-count to SKILL.md frontmatter config header.

findings.title: Stage 5 Search Strategy subsection is inferable
findings.detail: Four bullets on search strategy ('Search by domain first, then capabilities') that the LLM would follow naturally. ~40 tokens prunable.

findings.title: Stage 7 Cost Optimization duplicates Stage 4 model-tier guidance
findings.detail: Model selection and delegation depth guidance appears in both Stage 4 (runtime config) and Stage 7 (budget optimization). ~60 tokens of cross-stage duplication.

findings.title: Stage 9 validation checks duplicate script logic
findings.detail: Validation key checks listed in the prompt partially duplicate what validate-package.py already checks. ~30 tokens. The script output should be the source of truth.

findings.title: Stage 8 role-pattern examples are inferable but improve proposals
findings.detail: Example routines per role (CEO=weekly review, Managers=daily standup) accelerate first proposals but are technically inferable. ~50 tokens. Borderline — keep for proposal quality.

findings.title: No structured config header in SKILL.md frontmatter
findings.detail: Frontmatter has name and description but lacks type, interaction-model, stage-count. Reduces scanner and tooling interop.

strengths.title: Excellent progressive disclosure architecture
strengths.detail: SKILL.md is an 851-token routing hub; all stage logic lives in references/. The LLM never holds all 9 stages simultaneously. Per-turn token ceiling is ~1,850 worst case.

strengths.title: Consistent, self-contained stage templates
strengths.detail: Every stage follows Goal → How to Propose → Draft Update → Progression. Each stage is fully self-contained — no stage needs to read another to function.

strengths.title: Correct intelligence placement
strengths.detail: Deterministic work (assembly, validation) delegated to scripts. Creative/adaptive work (org design, instruction writing, budget strategy) stays in prompts. Fallback pattern included for environments without Python.

strengths.title: Strong HITL design philosophy
strengths.detail: 'Suggest, don't interrogate' is implemented throughout. Every stage proposes complete solutions, not questionnaires. Design Principles section is compact, with all four principles load-bearing.

strengths.title: Complete cross-reference integrity
strengths.detail: All 9 stage file paths resolve correctly. Progression chains are consistent. Inter-stage references (scripts, domain ref) all verified. Zero orphaned files.

strengths.title: Clean path standards
strengths.detail: Zero findings from path-standards scanner. No double prefixes, no bare internal paths, no absolute paths, no relative-prefix issues.

strengths.title: Sound dependency chain with no artificial bottlenecks
strengths.detail: Stage ordering forms a proper DAG. Sequential progression is justified by both data dependencies and HITL decision coherence. No stage could be moved earlier without losing input data.

strengths.title: Well-structured scripts with test coverage
strengths.detail: Both Python scripts (assemble-package.py, validate-package.py) have proper exit codes, uv run shebang, dependency declarations, and companion test files.

strengths.title: Compact, load-bearing domain reference
strengths.detail: paperclip-domain.md (204 lines) is entirely load-bearing — exact field names, types, enum values, package format specs. Zero narrative filler.

structure.assessment: Structurally complete. SKILL.md frontmatter valid, all 7 required sections present, all 9 stage files exist in references/ with correct paths. The 10 prepass findings (9 critical missing-stage + 1 medium naming) are all false positives — prepass searched skill root instead of references/. No template artifacts, no TODOs, no orphaned files. Config integration is proper with sensible defaults and draft persistence.

findings.title: Prepass false positives: 9 missing-stage + 1 naming convention
findings.detail: All 10 workflow-integrity prepass findings are false positives. Scanner searched skill root for stage files instead of references/ subdirectory. Naming convention flag is also incorrect — bmad-* prefix not required for standalone skills.

craft.assessment: SKILL.md scores 9/10 for progressive disclosure. Token efficiency is strong at ~210 prunable tokens out of ~8,700 total (2.4%). Outcome-driven balance is correct — outcomes for creative work, implementation rails for format-sensitive work. The main craft gap is absent config header for tooling interop and implicit domain reference loading strategy.

findings.title: Domain reference loading strategy is implicit
findings.detail: The domain reference is listed but there's no explicit instruction on when to load it. An eager agent might load the ~800-token file every stage. Should specify lazy-loading for stages 3 and 9.

cohesion.assessment: Stage flow coherence is strong — clean DAG with logical abstract-to-concrete progression. Nine stages match the Paperclip domain surface area. Purpose alignment has a gap: assembly output doesn't reflect all stage work. Design principles are honored in spirit but two (capture-don't-interrupt, soft gates) are not reinforced in stage files. Redundancy between stages is complementary, not duplicative.

stage_flow.notes: Clean DAG, no circular deps, every stage narrows the design space. Vision → Identity → Structure → Config → Skills → Goals → Budget → Scheduling → Assembly.

purpose_alignment.notes: Principles honored in implementation but assembly output gap means stages 5-8 work doesn't fully materialize. Permissions ghost field in Stage 4.

complexity_appropriateness.notes: 9 stages defensible for the domain. Fewer would require more context-switching; more would add ceremony. No viable merge candidates.

findings.title: Stage 4→5 feedback loop risk
findings.detail: Discovering a skill in Stage 5 might reveal a capability the agent should have had defined in Stage 4. No explicit instruction to reconcile capabilities after skill discovery.

findings.title: No inter-agent communication design stage
findings.detail: Stage 3 designs reporting lines but there's no structured design for how agents communicate — message formats, escalation protocols, handoff conventions.

efficiency.assessment: Rated 92/100. Sequential inter-stage ordering is correct for HITL. Minor intra-stage parallelization opportunities exist: Stage 5 skill searches across agents (~5-15s), Stage 9 review reads, and activation config loads. No subagent delegation needed. Draft JSON pattern is efficient — compact JSON, incremental reads, compaction-safe. Scripts are appropriately fast for expected workload (5-7 agents).

findings.title: Stage 5 skill searches can be parallelized across agents
findings.detail: Skill discovery queries are fully independent. Parallel npx skills find calls for all agents would save ~5-15 seconds of serial CLI execution.

findings.title: Stage 9 review reads can be batched
findings.detail: After validation, directory tree listing, COMPANY.md content, and sample AGENTS.md content are independent reads that can be performed in parallel.

findings.title: Domain reference lazy-loading should be explicit
findings.detail: Domain reference (~800 tokens) is only needed at stages 3 and 9, but current wording is ambiguous. An eager agent might load it every stage, wasting ~1,200 tokens per unnecessary load.

experience.assessment: Strong for first-timers through stages 1-3 via propose-and-refine. Friction emerges at Stage 4 (technical complexity without context) and Stage 7 (no cost anchoring). Expert users frustrated by mandatory linear walk. Edge cases (solo agent, mega org) have no detection or adaptation. Hostile environments (no uv/npx) fail silently at stages 5 and 9.

journeys.summary: Excellent experience through stages 1-3 via propose-and-refine pattern. Friction at Stage 4 (technical config like temperature/model without contextualized explanations) and Stage 7 (no cost anchoring — 'Is $50/month a lot?'). Drop-off risk at Stage 4 marathon.
friction_points: Stage 4 technical config lacks contextualized explanations for first-timers
Stage 7 budget has no pricing benchmarks or analogies
Stage 4 per-agent review causes rubber-stamping by agent 5+

journeys.summary: Frustrating experience. Users who arrive with complete org specs must walk through 9 stages even though they've already done the thinking. No fast-track or pre-populate mechanism.
friction_points: No quick-start detection for structured input
Must explain things they already know
Drop-off risk at Stage 1 — feels like unnecessary ceremony
bright_spots: Quality of proposals when they do engage is high
Compaction recovery means they can resume if interrupted

journeys.summary: Stage 1 handles well — proposes a vision from whatever they share. But subsequent stages assume increasing clarity. By Stage 5, they don't know what skills agents need because they don't understand what agents do.
friction_points: Stage 5 skills discovery assumes user knows agent capabilities
bright_spots: Suggestive HITL approach carries confused users further than interrogative would
Full proposals with defaults reduce decision burden

journeys.summary: Solo-agent orgs get an awkward hierarchy stage. Mega-orgs (20+ agents) break Stage 4 into a 2-hour marathon. No-CEO requests hit a validation wall with no explanation.
friction_points: Solo agent: org chart stage feels pointless
Mega org: Stage 4 becomes 2-hour marathon with no batch processing
No-CEO request: fails validation without explaining why CEO is required
bright_spots: general role catch-all handles unusual role types

journeys.summary: No upfront environment verification. Users discover missing tools (uv, npx) at Stage 5 or Stage 9 — deep into the workflow. Fallback paths exist but feel like afterthoughts.
friction_points: No uv check until Stage 9 assembly attempt
No npx check until Stage 5 skills discovery
Manual assembly fallback is a large ask of the LLM
No network access makes skills discovery a dead end
bright_spots: Script fallback instructions exist in SKILL.md
Skills stage has manual URL fallback path

journeys.summary: No API, no headless mode, no batch capability. Must interact manually for every org. Hybrid review-only mode and full headless mode assessed as achievable.
friction_points: No headless/batch mode for scripted org creation
No template system for reusing approved designs
Must walk through all 9 interactive stages every time
bright_spots: Draft JSON as state file is already machine-parseable
Scripts are standalone and could be called directly with pre-built JSON

autonomous.notes: Fundamentally interactive by design (HITL at every gate). A hybrid review-only mode (pre-populate all stages, single combined review) is low-effort and high-value. Full headless mode is viable for power users with complete specs — needs input schema, validation, and default-filling. Draft JSON state file and standalone scripts provide a foundation.

findings.title: No environment detection on activation
findings.detail: Four external dependencies (uv, npx, skills CLI, Paperclip CLI) are assumed available with no upfront check. Failures surface deep in the workflow.

findings.title: skills.sh external dependency risk
findings.detail: Stage 5 hardcodes reliance on a third-party skill registry. If skills.sh changes its API or goes down, the stage partially fails. Fallback path reads as an afterthought.

findings.title: Budget stage lacks cost anchoring
findings.detail: Stage 7 asks agent to suggest a budget but provides no model pricing data, no historical benchmarks, no example budget profiles. The agent is guessing.

scripts.assessment: Two existing scripts are well-implemented with zero lint findings and companion tests. Two new scripts recommended: validate-draft.py (consolidates draft validation across all stages) and summarize-draft.py (compact summaries for recovery and context reduction). The highest-value opportunity is mid-workflow draft validation — catches errors 1–8 stages earlier than current flow.
scripts.token_savings: ~4,200–12,100 tokens per full run (deterministic validation) + ~500–2,000 tokens per compaction event (summary generation)

findings.title: validate-draft.py — unified mid-workflow validator
findings.detail: Consolidates 6 sub-findings: JSON schema, org tree integrity, issue prefix format, cron syntax, budget arithmetic, cross-reference validation. Progressive checks by stage threshold.

findings.title: summarize-draft.py — compact stage-aware summaries
findings.detail: Produces ~150-token compact summary instead of LLM parsing raw JSON (~500-2,000+ tokens). Two modes: stage-entry context extraction and compaction recovery summary.

findings.title: assemble-package.py lacks idempotency
findings.detail: Running twice overwrites without warning. No --force flag or backup mechanism. Also no partial assembly — one bad agent fails the whole run.

findings.title: slugify() drops unicode characters
findings.detail: 'ÜberTech' becomes 'bertech'. Should use a unicode-aware slugifier to handle international company/agent names.

recommendations.action: Create validate-draft.py mid-workflow validator with progressive stage-threshold checks (org tree at ≥3, budget arithmetic at ≥7, cron syntax at ≥8). Call after each stage gate approval.

Fix assembly output gap — either populate package directories with project/skill/routine files from draft data, or remove empty directories and clarify the runtime-seeding model in stage instructions.

Improve compaction recovery — add stageProgress to draft JSON, write recovery preamble templates, add save-and-quit messaging at every gate, reinforce design principles with one-line reminders in stage Progression sections.

Add batch-approval mode to Stage 4 — group agents by role tier (CEO solo, managers grouped, same-role workers batched), show progress indicators, offer template application after first of a role type.

Trim ~210 tokens of cross-stage prompt duplication — collapse Stage 5 search strategy, deduplicate model-tier advice between stages 4 and 7, replace Stage 9 validation list with script deferral, add config header to frontmatter.
Add environment detection on activation — silently check for uv and npx, adapt flow to skip skills search or plan for manual assembly if tools are missing.
Add quick-start mode for expert users — detect structured input on activation, offer to pre-populate draft and skip to a combined review pass instead of walking through all 9 stages.
